title: Latent Bayesian Optimization via Autoregressive Normalizing Flows

reviews.summary: The paper proposes NF-BO, a latent Bayesian optimization framework that replaces VAE-based encoders/decoders with an invertible normalizing-flow mapping (SeqFlow) between discrete sequences (e.g., SELFIES for molecules) and a continuous latent space, thereby eliminating the reconstruction-induced value discrepancy that plagues prior LBO approaches. SeqFlow uses an autoregressive flow on continuous token embeddings and a deterministic embedding-to-token left-inverse, ensuring exact reconstruction for any observed input. The paper also introduces Token-level Adaptive Candidate Sampling (TACS), a PMI-based per-token importance scheme that biases local trust-region search toward informative latent dimensions. Experiments on Guacamol and PMO benchmarks report consistent, often large, improvements over recent LBO baselines and a broad set of molecular optimization methods.
strengths: Introduces an invertible, one-to-one mapping from discrete sequences to latent space using an autoregressive normalizing flow (SeqFlow), addressing a long-standing misalignment/reconstruction-gap issue in LBO.
Provides a simple yet principled left-inverse via nearest-embedding decoding that guarantees perfect reconstruction for codes produced by encoding actual inputs (g(e_x) → g⁻¹(·) → e_x → x).
Proposes TACS, a token-level, PMI-driven sampling strategy for trust-region candidate generation that adaptively focuses perturbations on important latent tokens.
Evaluates across two widely used suites (Guacamol: 7 tasks, multiple budgets; PMO: 23 tasks) and compares against numerous strong baselines (LBO variants and non-BO methods).
Includes ablations showing the contribution of TACS and an analysis of candidate diversity.
Reports performance at both low-budget and high-budget regimes, with gains that are large and consistent.
Clear articulation of the “value discrepancy” problem with an illustrative figure and pipeline diagrams contrasting VAE-based LBO and NF-BO.
Mathematical statements (Propositions 1–2) concisely formalize the injectivity and left-inverse properties for the embedding-based mapping.
Resolving reconstruction-induced misalignment is central to making LBO dependable in discrete domains; the proposed approach is a conceptually clean and general solution.
Strong empirical results on standard molecular optimization suites indicate substantial potential impact for the community working on BO over discrete sequences and drug discovery.
weaknesses: The “perfect reconstruction” guarantee strictly holds on the manifold of codes produced by the encoder (z = g(e_x)). BO proposals generally explore off-manifold z; after decode (g⁻¹) and argmax, re-encoding g(e_x̃) may not equal the proposed z̃. The paper should more explicitly characterize this and quantify how often z̃ ≈ g(e_x̃) during search.

The likelihood model for sequences relies on a hard argmax/δ mapping and a constrained dequantization q′(v|x) that samples within Voronoi cells of embeddings. The ELBO and acceptance scheme are sketched but important details (normalization constant handling, unbiasedness/consistency) are deferred; the theoretical soundness and training stability of this construction deserve deeper treatment.

PMI-based token importance uses p(x|z) defined via δ-indicators (with an ε hack). This is brittle: without a proper continuous likelihood, PMI degenerates. The paper would benefit from a more principled relaxations (e.g., temperature-smoothed softmax over embeddings) and sensitivity analyses to ε.

No direct comparison to recent decoupled LSBO approaches that explicitly address misalignment without retraining (e.g., COWBOYS) or to inversion-based fixes (InvBO) on overlapping benchmarks. Since misalignment is the central issue, these baselines are important.
Missing quantitative analysis of the claimed elimination of value discrepancy for observed data (e.g., histograms of f(x) vs f(decode(encode(x))) for SeqFlow vs VAE on the same datasets).

Limited reporting on computational cost: training/inference time for SeqFlow vs. VAEs, impact on per-iteration BO wall-clock, scaling with sequence length/token set size, and SVGP configurations (e.g., inducing points).
TACS details are under-specified (e.g., precise perturbation distribution per token, trust-region geometry, κ/τ schedules), hindering reproducibility.
Some notation and probability definitions (e.g., Eq. (9)–(11), the acceptance probability, the exact form of p(x|v) used in PMI with ε) are ambiguous or only informally motivated.

The autoregressive flow architecture is largely in the appendix; a brief but concrete block description (couplings, conditioner networks, invertible transforms) in the main text would help readers assess capacity and constraints.
While Ziegler & Rush (2019) is cited, more discussion contrasting SeqFlow to discrete-sequence flows and flow-matching priors (e.g., MoltenFlow) would help contextualize choices.
Recent LBO critiques and alternatives (decoupling in GP space, inversion strategies) warrant empirical comparison and deeper discussion given the shared motivation.
reviews.questions:
1. Does the perfect-reconstruction guarantee hold only for z in the image of g(e_x)? In practice, how often do BO proposals satisfy z̃ ≈ g(e_x̃) after decode/round/re-encode, and how large is the typical discrepancy? Please provide statistics across benchmarks and discuss its impact on surrogate accuracy.
2. Can you report runtime and memory comparisons against VAE-based LBO (LOLBO/CoBO/PG-LBO) under identical hardware and budgets, including SeqFlow training time per iteration and SVGP training/prediction costs?
3. How exactly are latent tokens perturbed in TACS (distribution, step size, trust-region geometry)? Are τ and κ fixed or adapted during optimization? Please include a sensitivity analysis and implementation details sufficient for reproduction.
4. For PMI, what exact form of p(x|v) do you use (ε value, smoothing), and how many Monte Carlo samples are needed for stable estimates? Have you explored soft likelihoods (e.g., temperature-softened cosine) to avoid δ-induced degeneracy?
5. Could you add a direct comparison with InvBO (plug-in inversion) on Guacamol and at least one PMO regime, and with a decoupled GP-in-structure-space approach (e.g., Tanimoto-kernel BO or COWBOYS)? These are the most relevant alternatives given the motivation.
6. Do you have quantitative evidence (beyond the VAE histogram in Fig. 1) that SeqFlow eliminates value discrepancy for observed data across tasks (e.g., f(x) vs f(decode(encode(x))))? If so, please include.
7. How sensitive are results to the choice and initialization of the token embedding dictionary E and the embedding dimension F? Have you tried learned (task-aware) embeddings or using pretrained token embeddings?
8. What is the validity rate of decoded molecules across the search (especially when far from anchors), and how does it compare to VAE-based decoders and SELFIES-based GA baselines?
reviews.assessment: This paper tackles a central weakness of latent Bayesian optimization for discrete sequences—the reconstruction-induced value discrepancy—via a clean, principled use of invertible normalizing flows over token embeddings, yielding exact reconstruction for observed data and improving surrogate fidelity. The additional TACS mechanism is sensible and seems to help local search. Empirically, the gains on Guacamol and PMO are substantial and, if robust, will be of high interest to the ICLR community working on BO and molecular design. The main reservations are (i) incomplete clarity and theory around the discrete likelihood/PMI construction, (ii) the practical importance of off-manifold proposals where the exact left-inverse no longer holds, and (iii) missing comparisons to the most relevant recent alternatives (InvBO and decoupled GP-in-structure-space methods such as COWBOYS). Addressing these concerns—especially adding comparisons and reporting compute, off-manifold drift statistics, and clearer TACS details—would significantly strengthen the submission. Overall, I find the core idea strong, the empirical results compelling, and the potential impact high; with the requested clarifications and additions, this work would be a solid fit for ICLR.
